strands.title: System Context & Constraints
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. PCI-DSS in scope. Payments module heavily coupled: reaches into inventory, order state, user profiles. Auth session tokens stored in payments DB for uptime reasons (infrastructure coupling, not domain coupling). Last quarter: payments DB outage → auth failure → full application outage — this is the business trigger. CEO mandated auth independence from payments within 90 days. Team has a team lead (female) with strong opinions on architecture; team size/structure still unknown despite being asked repeatedly. 6-month overall timeline and limited budget introduced by assistant in cycle 1, never confirmed by user. Compliance officer constraints: any new auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live; two auth systems cannot run simultaneously (doubles audit surface). Auth code coupled to ORM layer (team lead's claim). Inventory team exists separately and claims to have a clean existing API.

key_claims.text: Monolith is 500K LOC Java on Tomcat
PCI-DSS is in scope
Payments module reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles
Auth session tokens stored in payments DB for uptime reasons
Payments DB outage last quarter cascaded into full application outage via auth
CEO mandated auth independent of payments within 90 days
New auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live
Cannot run two auth systems simultaneously per compliance
Auth code is coupled to ORM layer (team lead's claim)
6-month overall timeline and limited budget are confirmed constraints

strands.title: Migration Strategy & Architecture
strands.content: EVOLVED FROM FULL MICROSERVICES TO HYBRID: Modular monolith + payments-only extraction is now the recommended approach. Original strangler fig pattern via API gateway for full decomposition was reassessed when modular monolith question was raised. Key realization: only PCI scope reduction requires actual service extraction, and only for payments. Auth resilience solved by data layer separation. Independent deployability solved by feature flags. Split by business domain, not technical layer — this principle holds for both microservices and modular monolith. Each module owns its database from day one — THE single most important success factor regardless of architecture choice. Anti-patterns still apply: containerizing without decomposing, shared databases, technical-layer splits. CRITICAL RULE: extraction changes deployment boundary only — same language, DB engine, data model, business logic. Technology migrations (e.g., Postgres→DynamoDB) are separate follow-on decisions requiring data-driven justification. DATA OWNERSHIP is the ultimate success/failure determinant: 'Can I take down Service X's database with zero impact on other services?' is the month-6 test. Modular monolith with compile-time enforced boundaries (ArchUnit, Gradle subprojects, Java modules) provides data ownership without operational overhead of full microservices. Microservices-ready: any module can be extracted later when concrete scaling need arises. SELF-REFLECTION: Assistant acknowledges microservices bias — should have questioned the frame ('do you need microservices?') before proposing architecture. The modular monolith conversation should have been turn 2, not turn 12. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY delivered to CEO: 5 bullets covering problem framing (not 'pursuing microservices'), hybrid approach, 30-day incident fix, data ownership as non-negotiable, and honest 6-month/9-12 month timeline.

key_claims.text: Modular monolith + payments extraction is simpler and sufficient for stated business goals
Only PCI scope reduction requires actual microservice extraction
Data ownership boundaries are THE single most important success factor
Extraction changes deployment boundary only — no bundled technology migrations
Full microservices may still be needed if undisclosed scaling/team-structure requirements exist
Modular monolith is microservices-ready — modules can be extracted when concrete need arises
Executive summary successfully frames outcomes over architecture for CEO audience

strands.title: Auth Extraction & 90-Day Plan
strands.content: Sessions must migrate from payments DB to dedicated Redis (ElastiCache Multi-AZ) as FIRST action — urgent given CEO mandate and incident history. Pattern: dual-write to both stores, validate Redis reads, cut over, drop sessions table. Target: days 1-14 for Redis live. This alone eliminates the incident scenario and gives a day-14 CEO demo. Two 90-day targets: Target A (auth data independence, 60-70 days, lower risk) vs Target B (full auth service extraction, 90 days, achievable but stressful). RECOMMENDATION: Commit publicly to Target A, architect toward Target B. By day 30 incident scenario definitively eliminated. COMPLIANCE IMPACT: If extraction counts as 'new system,' full service extraction takes 13+ weeks (5 build + 6 audit + 2 remediation). Strategy: deliver data layer separation by day 30 (eliminates incident), submit for audit at day 31, build during audit window, target day 90-105 for full service go-live. GREENFIELD DEFINITIVELY REJECTED due to compliance constraint (6-week audit on new system + build time exceeds 90 days, and dual-system operation prohibited). REWRITE-IN-PLACE THEN EXTRACT is the only viable path: map ORM coupling (weeks 3-4), rewrite auth internals inside monolith behind clean AuthService interface using repository pattern (weeks 5-8), extract as standalone service (weeks 9-12). 3-week decision gate: if rewrite-in-place still too messy after 3 weeks, revisit with evidence. CUTOVER STRATEGY changed by compliance: no dual-run validation. Replace with: (a) traffic replay harness in staging, (b) feature-flag percentage routing (confirm with compliance ≠ simultaneous systems), (c) instant rollback via API gateway (<5 min). Sessions in payments DB may already be a PCI finding — migration is documentable risk reduction. MODULAR MONOLITH CONTEXT: If hybrid approach adopted, auth stays as a module in the monolith with clean boundaries and dedicated DB. No service extraction needed unless scaling demands it. This eliminates the compliance audit for auth entirely.

key_claims.text: Session migration to Redis resolves auth-payments coupling before extraction
Day-14 Redis migration eliminates the specific incident scenario
Target A (data independence) achievable in 60-70 days
Greenfield auth is definitively off the table due to compliance constraint
Rewrite-in-place then extract is the only viable path for full service
If modular monolith adopted, auth extraction as service may be unnecessary
Feature flag routing may not count as simultaneous systems (needs compliance confirmation)

strands.title: Payments, Inventory & Parallel Work
strands.content: PAYMENTS: Extract as standalone service (the one true microservice in hybrid approach). The tangling IS the argument for extraction — late extraction forces other services/modules to build around monolith payment state. PCI nervousness addressed by separating code risk from compliance scope: extraction shrinks audit surface from entire monolith to one bounded service (~80% scope reduction with processor vault). Pre-extraction: migrate to processor vault if not done (SAQ-D to SAQ A-EP). Sub-phases: ACL inside monolith (weeks 11-12), data isolation (weeks 13-15), network boundary extraction (weeks 16-18), PCI hardening + cutover (weeks 19-20). Actual payment surface ~20% of perceived — most tendrils are orchestration logic. Payments extraction will require security audit — start audit process during build phase. INVENTORY: 3-week Postgres extraction approved if API is clean. DynamoDB REJECTED for bundling with extraction — realistic DynamoDB estimate 8-12 weeks, not 3. Likely real need is Redis cache for hot SKU reads. MODULAR MONOLITH CONTEXT: inventory stays as module with clean boundaries unless scaling demands extraction. PARALLEL SERVICES TRACK (while auth runs): create InventoryService interface inside monolith (1-2 weeks, zero risk), separate inventory data access (2-3 weeks), map read vs write patterns (1 week analysis), document order state machine (3-5 days), identify domain events (1 week), extract notifications as first real extraction (2-3 weeks, proves event bus). Notification extraction is ideal first extraction: fire-and-forget, no PCI/auth scope, forces event bus to be built. Priority if bandwidth limited: inventory interface > order state machine docs > domain events > notification extraction. CONSISTENCY MODEL: Payments team's concern about eventual consistency is correct but addressed by hybrid architecture. Strong consistency is per-service, not cross-service. Payment charge+record is ACID within payments service boundary. Cross-boundary coordination uses three patterns: (1) idempotency keys on every payment call (prevents double-charge), (2) reservation timeouts (prevents phantom inventory locks, self-healing), (3) reconciliation job every 5 min (catches edge cases). In hybrid architecture, only ONE cross-service boundary exists (monolith→payments), so full saga pattern is unnecessary. Orders+inventory can still use local transactions within monolith. The best distributed transaction is the one you don't need.

key_claims.text: Payments coupling is the argument FOR early extraction
Extracting payments reduces PCI audit scope by ~80%
Actual payment surface is ~20% of perceived
DynamoDB bundled with extraction is scope creep — rejected
DynamoDB migration realistic estimate is 8-12 weeks, not 3
Notification extraction is viable as first low-risk service extraction
Services track work is entirely non-blocking on auth track
Hybrid architecture eliminates need for saga pattern — only one cross-service boundary
Idempotency + timeouts + reconciliation handles all realistic payment failure modes

strands.title: Honest Assessment & Success Criteria
strands.content: REASSESSED WITH MODULAR MONOLITH OPTION: If hybrid approach (modular monolith + payments extraction) adopted, 6-month timeline becomes comfortably achievable rather than tight. Original full-microservices assessment: auth+payments achievable, inventory likely, order fulfillment not in 6 months, realistic full timeline 9-12 months. HYBRID ASSESSMENT: month 1 foundation + auth data independence, months 2-3 modular monolith hardening with compile-time boundaries, months 3-5 payments extraction + PCI audit, months 5-6 payments live. All three CEO goals met within 6 months. MINIMUM VIABLE MIGRATION = auth data independence + payments as isolated PCI-compliant service + modular monolith with enforced boundaries. THREE FAILURE MODES: (1) audit pipeline serialization — less severe with only one extraction, (2) residual monolith orphaned — assign explicit ownership, (3) scope creep accumulation — enforce 'extraction changes one thing' rule. THE ONE THING that determines success or failure: data ownership boundaries. Every table has exactly one owning service/module. No shared databases, no cross-service JOINs. Month-6 test: 'Can I take down Service X's database with zero impact on other services?' If yes → success. If no → distributed monolith, worse than starting point. This principle applies equally whether architecture is full microservices or modular monolith. 6-month boundary reframed as: point where migration is complete (hybrid) or irreversible with remaining work being execution not invention (full microservices). SELF-REFLECTION ON PROCESS: Assistant changed mind on five things during conversation: (1) full microservices → hybrid, (2) extraction sequence invalidated by coupling topology, (3) consistency problem favors hybrid (avoids sagas entirely), (4) dual-run safety net was itself a compliance violation, (5) original 6-month timeline was optimistic by 3-4 months for full microservices. Meta-lesson: architecture community has microservices bias; should question the frame before proposing solutions. What held constant: data ownership principle, one-change-per-step rule, infrastructure-vs-domain coupling framework, incident-first prioritization. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY delivered: 5 bullets for CEO covering problem framing, hybrid approach, 30-day fix, data ownership, honest timeline. UNKNOWN RISKS IDENTIFIED: (1) external systems reading DB directly (BI tools, ETL, partner integrations), (2) poor test coverage enabling silent regressions, (3) undocumented DB triggers/views/stored procedures spanning domains, (4) feature development creating new couplings during migration, (5) key person risk. Priority action: run DB connection audit and DB object queries before any migration work starts.

key_claims.text: Hybrid approach makes 6-month timeline comfortably achievable
Full microservices: order fulfillment extraction not achievable in 6 months
Full monolith decommission takes 9-12 months under microservices approach
Data ownership boundaries are the single success/failure determinant
Modular monolith + payments extraction meets all three stated CEO goals
Assistant's initial microservices recommendation was premature — should have questioned frame first
Executive summary successfully captures key decisions for CEO audience
External systems reading DB directly is the highest-likelihood unknown risk
Feature development during migration will create new couplings without ArchUnit enforcement

open_questions: CRITICAL (architecture-determining): Is there a specific reason full microservices was assumed from the start — a scaling requirement, team structure mandate, or something else? The answer determines whether hybrid (modular monolith + payments) or full microservices is the right path.
CRITICAL (compliance): Does extracting the existing auth system constitute a 'new auth system' for audit purposes? (Only relevant if full microservices path chosen — modular monolith sidesteps this.)
What is the team size and structure? How many engineers total? (Asked repeatedly, still unanswered — critical for feasibility of parallel tracks)
How clean is the inventory team's 'existing API'? REST endpoints with defined contracts, or internal method calls dressed up as an API?
What payment processor is in use? Is card data already tokenized via processor vault?
What does the auth code actually look like — is the ORM coupling an implementation problem (fixable by refactoring) or a model problem (sessions vs JWT)?
instructions_for_next: The conversation has now reached its natural conclusion with the executive summary delivered. All major architectural topics have been covered including a full self-reflection on how thinking evolved. Key settled decisions: modular monolith + payments-only extraction (hybrid), data ownership as success criterion, extraction changes one thing only, no DynamoDB bundling, sessions to Redis first, payments extraction with PCI scope reduction, hybrid rewrite approach for auth, idempotency+timeouts+reconciliation for consistency. The executive summary frames the entire conversation for the CEO in 5 bullets. If the conversation continues, likely topics: implementation details for specific phases, team staffing decisions, compliance officer conversations, or new complications. The modular monolith recommendation is the biggest open architectural fork — user hasn't explicitly confirmed which path. Team size remains THE critical unknown. The self-reflection establishes that the assistant recognizes its own microservices bias and would approach differently if starting over. The executive summary is the capstone deliverable of this planning session.
